title: VI
html:
VI

No, Trina did not know. ‚ÄúDo I love him? Do I love him?‚Äù A thousand times she put the question to herself during the next two or three days. At night she hardly slept, but lay broad awake for hours in her little, gayly painted bed, with its white netting, torturing herself with doubts and questions. At times she remembered the scene in the station with a veritable agony of shame, and at other times she was ashamed to recall it with a thrill of joy. Nothing could have been more sudden, more unexpected, than that surrender of herself. For over a year she had thought that Marcus would some day be her husband. They would be married, she supposed, some time in the future, she did not know exactly when; the matter did not take definite shape in her mind. She liked Cousin Mark very well. And then suddenly this crosscurrent had set in; this blond giant had appeared, this huge, stolid fellow, with his immense, crude strength. She had not loved him at first, that was certain. The day he had spoken to her in his Parlors she had only been terrified. If he had confined himself to merely speaking, as did Marcus, to pleading with her, to wooing her at a distance, forestalling her wishes, showing her little attentions, sending her boxes of candy, she could have easily withstood him. But he had only to take her in his arms, to crush down her struggle with his enormous strength, to subdue her, conquer her by sheer brute force, and she gave up in an instant.

But whyÔªø‚Äîwhy had she done so? Why did she feel the desire, the necessity of being conquered by a superior strength? Why did it please her? Why had it suddenly thrilled her from head to foot with a quick, terrifying gust of passion, the like of which she had never known? Never at his best had Marcus made her feel like that, and yet she had always thought she cared for Cousin Mark more than for anyone else.

When McTeague had all at once caught her in his huge arms, something had leaped to life in herÔªø‚Äîsomething that had hitherto lain dormant, something strong and overpowering. It frightened her now as she thought of it, this second self that had wakened within her, and that shouted and clamored for recognition. And yet, was it to be feared? Was it something to be ashamed of? Was it not, after all, natural, clean, spontaneous? Trina knew that she was a pure girl; knew that this sudden commotion within her carried with it no suggestion of vice.

Dimly, as figures seen in a waking dream, these ideas floated through Trina‚Äôs mind. It was quite beyond her to realize them clearly; she could not know what they meant. Until that rainy day by the shore of the bay Trina had lived her life with as little self-consciousness as a tree. She was frank, straightforward, a healthy, natural human being, without sex as yet. She was almost like a boy. At once there had been a mysterious disturbance. The woman within her suddenly awoke.

Did she love McTeague? Difficult question. Did she choose him for better or for worse, deliberately, of her own free will, or was Trina herself allowed even a choice in the taking of that step that was to make or mar her life? The Woman is awakened, and, starting from her sleep, catches blindly at what first her newly opened eyes light upon. It is a spell, a witchery, ruled by chance alone, inexplicableÔªø‚Äîa fairy queen enamored of a clown with ass‚Äôs ears.

McTeague had awakened the Woman, and, whether she would or no, she was his now irrevocably; struggle against it as she would, she belonged to him, body and soul, for life or for death. She had not sought it, she had not desired it. The spell was laid upon her. Was it a blessing? Was it a curse? It was all one; she was his, indissolubly, for evil or for good.

And he? The very act of submission that bound the woman to him forever had made her seem less desirable in his eyes. Their undoing had already begun. Yet neither of them was to blame. From the first they had not sought each other. Chance had brought them face to face, and mysterious instincts as ungovernable as the winds of heaven were at work knitting their lives together. Neither of them had asked that this thing should beÔªø‚Äîthat their destinies, their very souls, should be the sport of chance. If they could have known, they would have shunned the fearful risk. But they were allowed no voice in the matter. Why should it all be?

It had been on a Wednesday that the scene in the B Street station had taken place. Throughout the rest of the week, at every hour of the day, Trina asked herself the same question: ‚ÄúDo I love him? Do I really love him? Is this what love is like?‚Äù As she recalled McTeagueÔªø‚Äîrecalled his huge, square-cut head, his salient jaw, his shock of yellow hair, his heavy, lumbering body, his slow witsÔªø‚Äîshe found little to admire in him beyond his physical strength, and at such moments she shook her head decisively. ‚ÄúNo, surely she did not love him.‚Äù Sunday afternoon, however, McTeague called. Trina had prepared a little speech for him. She was to tell him that she did not know what had been the matter with her that Wednesday afternoon; that she had acted like a bad girl; that she did not love him well enough to marry him; that she had told him as much once before.

McTeague saw her alone in the little front parlor. The instant she appeared he came straight towards her. She saw what he was bent upon doing. ‚ÄúWait a minute,‚Äù she cried, putting out her hands. ‚ÄúWait. You don‚Äôt understand. I have got something to say to you.‚Äù She might as well have talked to the wind. McTeague put aside her hands with a single gesture, and gripped her to him in a bearlike embrace that all but smothered her. Trina was but a reed before that giant strength. McTeague turned her face to his and kissed her again upon the mouth. Where was all Trina‚Äôs resolve then? Where was her carefully prepared little speech? Where was all her hesitation and torturing doubts of the last few days? She clasped McTeague‚Äôs huge red neck with both her slender arms; she raised her adorable little chin and kissed him in return, exclaiming: ‚ÄúOh, I do love you! I do love you!‚Äù Never afterward were the two so happy as at that moment.

A little later in that same week, when Marcus and McTeague were taking lunch at the car conductors‚Äô coffee-joint, the former suddenly exclaimed:

‚ÄúSay, Mac, now that you‚Äôve got Trina, you ought to do more for her. By damn! you ought to, for a fact. Why don‚Äôt you take her out somewhereÔªø‚Äîto the theatre, or somewhere? You ain‚Äôt on to your job.‚Äù

Naturally, McTeague had told Marcus of his success with Trina. Marcus had taken on a grand air.

‚ÄúYou‚Äôve got her, have you? Well, I‚Äôm glad of it, old man. I am, for a fact. I know you‚Äôll be happy with her. I know how I would have been. I forgive you; yes, I forgive you, freely.‚Äù

McTeague had not thought of taking Trina to the theatre.

‚ÄúYou think I ought to, Mark?‚Äù he inquired, hesitating. Marcus answered, with his mouth full of suet pudding:

‚ÄúWhy, of course. That‚Äôs the proper caper.‚Äù

‚ÄúWellÔªø‚Äîwell, that‚Äôs so. The theatreÔªø‚Äîthat‚Äôs the word.‚Äù

‚ÄúTake her to the variety show at the Orpheum. There‚Äôs a good show there this week; you‚Äôll have to take Mrs.¬ÝSieppe, too, of course,‚Äù he added. Marcus was not sure of himself as regarded certain proprieties, nor, for that matter, were any of the people of the little world of Polk Street. The shop girls, the plumbers‚Äô apprentices, the small tradespeople, and their like, whose social position was not clearly defined, could never be sure how far they could go and yet preserve their ‚Äúrespectability.‚Äù When they wished to be ‚Äúproper,‚Äù they invariably overdid the thing. It was not as if they belonged to the ‚Äútough‚Äù element, who had no appearances to keep up. Polk Street rubbed elbows with the ‚Äúavenue‚Äù one block above. There were certain limits which its dwellers could not overstep; but unfortunately for them, these limits were poorly defined. They could never be sure of themselves. At an unguarded moment they might be taken for ‚Äútoughs,‚Äù so they generally erred in the other direction, and were absurdly formal. No people have a keener eye for the amenities than those whose social position is not assured.

‚ÄúOh, sure, you‚Äôll have to take her mother,‚Äù insisted Marcus. ‚ÄúIt wouldn‚Äôt be the proper racket if you didn‚Äôt.‚Äù

McTeague undertook the affair. It was an ordeal. Never in his life had he been so perturbed, so horribly anxious. He called upon Trina the following Wednesday and made arrangements. Mrs.¬ÝSieppe asked if little August might be included. It would console him for the loss of his steamboat.

‚ÄúSure, sure,‚Äù said McTeague. ‚ÄúAugust tooÔªø‚Äîeverybody,‚Äù he added, vaguely.

‚ÄúWe always have to leave so early,‚Äù complained Trina, ‚Äúin order to catch the last boat. Just when it‚Äôs becoming interesting.‚Äù

At this McTeague, acting upon a suggestion of Marcus Schouler‚Äôs, insisted they should stay at the flat over night. Marcus and the dentist would give up their rooms to them and sleep at the dog hospital. There was a bed there in the sick ward that old Grannis sometimes occupied when a bad case needed watching. All at once McTeague had an idea, a veritable inspiration.

‚ÄúAnd we‚ÄôllÔªø‚Äîwe‚ÄôllÔªø‚Äîwe‚Äôll haveÔªø‚Äîwhat‚Äôs the matter with having something to eat afterward in my Parlors?‚Äù

‚ÄúVairy goot,‚Äù commented Mrs.¬ÝSieppe. ‚ÄúBier, eh? And some damales.‚Äù

‚ÄúOh, I love tamales!‚Äù exclaimed Trina, clasping her hands.

McTeague returned to the city, rehearsing his instructions over and over. The theatre party began to assume tremendous proportions. First of all, he was to get the seats, the third or fourth row from the front, on the left-hand side, so as to be out of the hearing of the drums in the orchestra; he must make arrangements about the rooms with Marcus, must get in the beer, but not the tamales; must buy for himself a white lawn tieÔªø‚Äîso Marcus directed; must look to it that Maria Macapa put his room in perfect order; and, finally, must meet the Sieppes at the ferry slip at half-past seven the following Monday night.

The real labor of the affair began with the buying of the tickets. At the theatre McTeague got into wrong entrances; was sent from one wicket to another; was bewildered, confused; misunderstood directions; was at one moment suddenly convinced that he had not enough money with him, and started to return home. Finally he found himself at the box-office wicket.

‚ÄúIs it here you buy your seats?‚Äù

‚ÄúHow many?‚Äù

‚ÄúIs it hereÔªø‚Äî‚Äù

‚ÄúWhat night do you want ‚Äôem? Yes, sir, here‚Äôs the place.‚Äù

McTeague gravely delivered himself of the formula he had been reciting for the last dozen hours.

‚ÄúI want four seats for Monday night in the fourth row from the front, and on the right-hand side.‚Äù

‚ÄúRight hand as you face the house or as you face the stage?‚Äù McTeague was dumbfounded.

‚ÄúI want to be on the right-hand side,‚Äù he insisted, stolidly; adding, ‚Äúin order to be away from the drums.‚Äù

‚ÄúWell, the drums are on the right of the orchestra as you face the stage,‚Äù shouted the other impatiently; ‚Äúyou want to the left, then, as you face the house.‚Äù

‚ÄúI want to be on the right-hand side,‚Äù persisted the dentist.

Without a word the seller threw out four tickets with a magnificent, supercilious gesture.

‚ÄúThere‚Äôs four seats on the right-hand side, then, and you‚Äôre right up against the drums.‚Äù

‚ÄúBut I don‚Äôt want to be near the drums,‚Äù protested McTeague, beginning to perspire.

‚ÄúDo you know what you want at all?‚Äù said the ticket seller with calmness, thrusting his head at McTeague. The dentist knew that he had hurt this young man‚Äôs feelings.

‚ÄúI wantÔªø‚ÄîI want,‚Äù he stammered. The seller slammed down a plan of the house in front of him and began to explain excitedly. It was the one thing lacking to complete McTeague‚Äôs confusion.

‚ÄúThere are your seats,‚Äù finished the seller, shoving the tickets into McTeague‚Äôs hands. ‚ÄúThey are the fourth row from the front, and away from the drums. Now are you satisfied?‚Äù

‚ÄúAre they on the right-hand side? I want on the rightÔªø‚Äîno, I want on the left. I wantÔªø‚ÄîI don‚Äô know, I don‚Äô know.‚Äù

The seller roared. McTeague moved slowly away, gazing stupidly at the blue slips of pasteboard. Two girls took his place at the wicket. In another moment McTeague came back, peering over the girls‚Äô shoulders and calling to the seller:

‚ÄúAre these for Monday night?‚Äù

The other disdained reply. McTeague retreated again timidly, thrusting the tickets into his immense wallet. For a moment he stood thoughtful on the steps of the entrance. Then all at once he became enraged, he did not know exactly why; somehow he felt himself slighted. Once more he came back to the wicket.

‚ÄúYou can‚Äôt make small of me,‚Äù he shouted over the girls‚Äô shoulders; ‚ÄúyouÔªø‚Äîyou can‚Äôt make small of me. I‚Äôll thump you in the head, you littleÔªø‚Äîyou littleÔªø‚Äîyou littleÔªø‚ÄîlittleÔªø‚Äîlittle pup.‚Äù The ticket seller shrugged his shoulders wearily. ‚ÄúA dollar and a half,‚Äù he said to the two girls.

McTeague glared at him and breathed loudly. Finally he decided to let the matter drop. He moved away, but on the steps was once more seized with a sense of injury and outraged dignity.

‚ÄúYou can‚Äôt make small of me,‚Äù he called back a last time, wagging his head and shaking his fist. ‚ÄúI willÔªø‚ÄîI willÔªø‚ÄîI willÔªø‚Äîyes, I will.‚Äù He went off muttering.

At last Monday night came. McTeague met the Sieppes at the ferry, dressed in a black Prince Albert coat and his best slate-blue trousers, and wearing the made-up lawn necktie that Marcus had selected for him. Trina was very pretty in the black dress that McTeague knew so well. She wore a pair of new gloves. Mrs.¬ÝSieppe had on lisle-thread mits, and carried two bananas and an orange in a net reticule. ‚ÄúFor Owgooste,‚Äù she confided to him. Owgooste was in a Fauntleroy ‚Äúcostume‚Äù very much too small for him. Already he had been crying.

‚ÄúWoult you pelief, Doktor, dot bube has torn his stockun alreatty? Walk in der front, you; stop cryun. Where is dot berliceman?‚Äù

At the door of the theatre McTeague was suddenly seized with a panic terror. He had lost the tickets. He tore through his pockets, ransacked his wallet. They were nowhere to be found. All at once he remembered, and with a gasp of relief removed his hat and took them out from beneath the sweatband.

The party entered and took their places. It was absurdly early. The lights were all darkened, the ushers stood under the galleries in groups, the empty auditorium echoing with their noisy talk. Occasionally a waiter with his tray and clean white apron sauntered up and doun the aisle. Directly in front of them was the great iron curtain of the stage, painted with all manner of advertisements. From behind this came a noise of hammering and of occasional loud voices.

While waiting they studied their programmes. First was an overture by the orchestra, after which came ‚ÄúThe Gleasons, in their mirth-moving musical farce, entitled ‚ÄòMcMonnigal‚Äôs Courtship.‚Äô‚Ää‚Äù This was to be followed by ‚ÄúThe Lamont Sisters, Winnie and Violet, serio-comiques and skirt dancers.‚Äù And after this came a great array of other ‚Äúartists‚Äù and ‚Äúspecialty performers,‚Äù musical wonders, acrobats, lightning artists, ventriloquists, and last of all, ‚ÄúThe feature of the evening, the crowning scientific achievement of the nineteenth century, the kinetoscope.‚Äù McTeague was excited, dazzled. In five years he had not been twice to the theatre. Now he beheld himself inviting his ‚Äúgirl‚Äù and her mother to accompany him. He began to feel that he was a man of the world. He ordered a cigar.

Meanwhile the house was filling up. A few side brackets were turned on. The ushers ran up and down the aisles, stubs of tickets between their thumb and finger, and from every part of the auditorium could be heard the sharp clap-clapping of the seats as the ushers flipped them down. A buzz of talk arose. In the gallery a street gamin whistled shrilly, and called to some friends on the other side of the house.

‚ÄúAre they go-wun to begin pretty soon, ma?‚Äù whined Owgooste for the fifth or sixth time; adding, ‚ÄúSay, ma, can‚Äôt I have some candy?‚Äù A cadaverous little boy had appeared in their aisle, chanting, ‚ÄúCandies, French mixed candies, popcorn, peanuts and candy.‚Äù The orchestra entered, each man crawling out from an opening under the stage, hardly larger than the gate of a rabbit hutch. At every instant now the crowd increased; there were but few seats that were not taken. The waiters hurried up and down the aisles, their trays laden with beer glasses. A smell of cigar-smoke filled the air, and soon a faint blue haze rose from all corners of the house.

‚ÄúMa, when are they go-wun to begin?‚Äù cried Owgooste. As he spoke the iron advertisement curtain rose, disclosing the curtain proper underneath. This latter curtain was quite an affair. Upon it was painted a wonderful picture. A flight of marble steps led down to a stream of water; two white swans, their necks arched like the capital letter S, floated about. At the head of the marble steps were two vases filled with red and yellow flowers, while at the foot was moored a gondola. This gondola was full of red velvet rugs that hung over the side and trailed in the water. In the prow of the gondola a young man in vermilion tights held a mandolin in his left hand, and gave his right to a girl in white satin. A King Charles spaniel, dragging a leading-string in the shape of a huge pink sash, followed the girl. Seven scarlet roses were scattered upon the two lowest steps, and eight floated in the water.

‚ÄúAin‚Äôt that pretty, Mac?‚Äù exclaimed Trina, turning to the dentist.

‚ÄúMa, ain‚Äôt they go-wun to begin now-wow?‚Äù whined Owgooste. Suddenly the lights all over the house blazed up. ‚ÄúAh!‚Äù said everybody all at once.

‚ÄúAin‚Äôt ut crowdut?‚Äù murmured Mr.¬ÝSieppe. Every seat was taken; many were even standing up.

‚ÄúI always like it better when there is a crowd,‚Äù said Trina. She was in great spirits that evening. Her round, pale face was positively pink.

The orchestra banged away at the overture, suddenly finishing with a great flourish of violins. A short pause followed. Then the orchestra played a quickstep strain, and the curtain rose on an interior furnished with two red chairs and a green sofa. A girl in a short blue dress and black stockings entered in a hurry and began to dust the two chairs. She was in a great temper, talking very fast, disclaiming against the ‚Äúnew lodger.‚Äù It appeared that this latter never paid his rent; that he was given to late hours. Then she came down to the footlights and began to sing in a tremendous voice, hoarse and flat, almost like a man‚Äôs. The chorus, of a feeble originality, ran:

Oh, how happy I will be,
When my darling‚Äôs face I‚Äôll see;
Oh, tell him for to meet me in the moonlight,
Down where the golden lilies bloom.

The orchestra played the tune of this chorus a second time, with certain variations, while the girl danced to it. She sidled to one side of the stage and kicked, then sidled to the other and kicked again. As she finished with the song, a man, evidently the lodger in question, came in. Instantly McTeague exploded in a roar of laughter. The man was intoxicated, his hat was knocked in, one end of his collar was unfastened and stuck up into his face, his watch-chain dangled from his pocket, and a yellow satin slipper was tied to a buttonhole of his vest; his nose was vermilion, one eye was black and blue. After a short dialogue with the girl, a third actor appeared. He was dressed like a little boy, the girl‚Äôs younger brother. He wore an immense turned-down collar, and was continually doing handsprings and wonderful back somersaults. The ‚Äúact‚Äù devolved upon these three people; the lodger making love to the girl in the short blue dress, the boy playing all manner of tricks upon him, giving him tremendous digs in the ribs or slaps upon the back that made him cough, pulling chairs from under him, running on all fours between his legs and upsetting him, knocking him over at inopportune moments. Every one of his falls was accentuated by a bang upon the bass drum. The whole humor of the ‚Äúact‚Äù seemed to consist in the tripping up of the intoxicated lodger.

This horseplay delighted McTeague beyond measure. He roared and shouted every time the lodger went down, slapping his knee, wagging his head. Owgooste crowed shrilly, clapping his hands and continually asking, ‚ÄúWhat did he say, ma? What did he say?‚Äù Mrs.¬ÝSieppe laughed immoderately, her huge fat body shaking like a mountain of jelly. She exclaimed from time to time, ‚ÄúAch, Gott, dot fool!‚Äù Even Trina was moved, laughing demurely, her lips closed, putting one hand with its new glove to her mouth.

The performance went on. Now it was the ‚Äúmusical marvels,‚Äù two men extravagantly made up as negro minstrels, with immense shoes and plaid vests. They seemed to be able to wrestle a tune out of almost anythingÔªø‚Äîglass bottles, cigar-box fiddles, strings of sleigh-bells, even graduated brass tubes, which they rubbed with resined fingers. McTeague was stupefied with admiration.

‚ÄúThat‚Äôs what you call musicians,‚Äù he announced gravely. ‚Äú‚Ää‚ÄôHome, Sweet Home,‚Äô played upon a trombone. Think of that! Art could go no farther.‚Äù

The acrobats left him breathless. They were dazzling young men with beautifully parted hair, continually making graceful gestures to the audience. In one of them the dentist fancied he saw a strong resemblance to the boy who had tormented the intoxicated lodger and who had turned such marvellous somersaults. Trina could not bear to watch their antics. She turned away her head with a little shudder. ‚ÄúIt always makes me sick,‚Äù she explained.

The beautiful young lady, ‚ÄúThe Society Contralto,‚Äù in evening dress, who sang the sentimental songs, and carried the sheets of music at which she never looked, pleased McTeague less. Trina, however, was captivated. She grew pensive over

You do not love meÔªø‚Äîno;
Bid me goodbye and go;

and split her new gloves in her enthusiasm when it was finished.

‚ÄúDon‚Äôt you love sad music, Mac?‚Äù she murmured.

Then came the two comedians. They talked with fearful rapidity; their wit and repartee seemed inexhaustible.

‚ÄúAs I was going down the street yesterdayÔªø‚Äî‚Äù

‚ÄúAh! as you were going down the streetÔªø‚Äîall right.‚Äù

‚ÄúI saw a girl at a windowÔªø‚Äî‚Äù

‚ÄúYou saw a girl at a window.‚Äù

‚ÄúAnd this girl she was a corkerÔªø‚Äî‚Äù

‚ÄúAh! as you were going down the street yesterday you saw a girl at a window, and this girl she was a corker. All right, go on.‚Äù

The other comedian went on. The joke was suddenly evolved. A certain phrase led to a song, which was sung with lightning rapidity, each performer making precisely the same gestures at precisely the same instant. They were irresistible. McTeague, though he caught but a third of the jokes, could have listened all night.

After the comedians had gone out, the iron advertisement curtain was let down.

‚ÄúWhat comes now?‚Äù said McTeague, bewildered.

‚ÄúIt‚Äôs the intermission of fifteen minutes now.‚Äù

The musicians disappeared through the rabbit hutch, and the audience stirred and stretched itself. Most of the young men left their seats.

During this intermission McTeague and his party had ‚Äúrefreshments.‚Äù Mrs.¬ÝSieppe and Trina had Queen Charlottes, McTeague drank a glass of beer, Owgooste ate the orange and one of the bananas. He begged for a glass of lemonade, which was finally given him.

‚ÄúJoost to geep um quiet,‚Äù observed Mrs.¬ÝSieppe.

But almost immediately after drinking his lemonade Owgooste was seized with a sudden restlessness. He twisted and wriggled in his seat, swinging his legs violently, looking about him with eyes full of a vague distress. At length, just as the musicians were returning, he stood up and whispered energetically in his mother‚Äôs ear. Mrs.¬ÝSieppe was exasperated at once.

‚ÄúNo, no,‚Äù she cried, reseating him brusquely.

The performance was resumed. A lightning artist appeared, drawing caricatures and portraits with incredible swiftness. He even went so far as to ask for subjects from the audience, and the names of prominent men were shouted to him from the gallery. He drew portraits of the President, of Grant, of Washington, of Napoleon Bonaparte, of Bismarck, of Garibaldi, of P. T. Barnum.

And so the evening passed. The hall grew very hot, and the smoke of innumerable cigars made the eyes smart. A thick blue mist hung low over the heads of the audience. The air was full of varied smellsÔªø‚Äîthe smell of stale cigars, of flat beer, of orange peel, of gas, of sachet powders, and of cheap perfumery.

One ‚Äúartist‚Äù after another came upon the stage. McTeague‚Äôs attention never wandered for a minute. Trina and her mother enjoyed themselves hugely. At every moment they made comments to one another, their eyes never leaving the stage.

‚ÄúAin‚Äôt dot fool joost too funny?‚Äù

‚ÄúThat‚Äôs a pretty song. Don‚Äôt you like that kind of a song?‚Äù

‚ÄúWonderful! It‚Äôs wonderful! Yes, yes, wonderful! That‚Äôs the word.‚Äù

Owgooste, however, lost interest. He stood up in his place, his back to the stage, chewing a piece of orange peel and watching a little girl in her father‚Äôs lap across the aisle, his eyes fixed in a glassy, ox-like stare. But he was uneasy. He danced from one foot to the other, and at intervals appealed in hoarse whispers to his mother, who disdained an answer.

‚ÄúMa, say, ma-ah,‚Äù he whined, abstractedly chewing his orange peel, staring at the little girl.

‚ÄúMa-ah, say, ma.‚Äù At times his monotonous plaint reached his mother‚Äôs consciousness. She suddenly realized what this was that was annoying her.

‚ÄúOwgooste, will you sit down?‚Äù She caught him up all at once, and jammed him down into his place. ‚ÄúBe quiet, den; loog; listun at der yunge girls.‚Äù

Three young women and a young man who played a zither occupied the stage. They were dressed in Tyrolese costume; they were yodlers, and sang in German about ‚Äúmountain tops‚Äù and ‚Äúbold hunters‚Äù and the like. The yodling chorus was a marvel of flute-like modulations. The girls were really pretty, and were not made up in the least. Their ‚Äúturn‚Äù had a great success. Mrs.¬ÝSieppe was entranced. Instantly she remembered her girlhood and her native Swiss village.

‚ÄúAch, dot is heavunly; joost like der old country. Mein gran‚Äômutter used to be one of der mos‚Äô famous yodlers. When I was leedle, I haf seen dem joost like dat.‚Äù

‚ÄúMa-ah,‚Äù began Owgooste fretfully, as soon as the yodlers had departed. He could not keep still an instant; he twisted from side to side, swinging his legs with incredible swiftness.

‚ÄúMa-ah, I want to go ho-ome.‚Äù

‚ÄúPehave!‚Äù exclaimed his mother, shaking him by the arm; ‚Äúloog, der leedle girl is watchun you. Dis is der last dime I take you to der blay, you see.‚Äù

‚ÄúI don‚Äôt ca-are; I‚Äôm sleepy.‚Äù At length, to their great relief, he went to sleep, his head against his mother‚Äôs arm.

The kinetoscope fairly took their breaths away.

‚ÄúWhat will they do next?‚Äù observed Trina, in amazement. ‚ÄúAin‚Äôt that wonderful, Mac?‚Äù

McTeague was awestruck.

‚ÄúLook at that horse move his head,‚Äù he cried excitedly, quite carried away. ‚ÄúLook at that cable car comingÔªø‚Äîand the man going across the street. See, here comes a truck. Well, I never in all my life! What would Marcus say to this?‚Äù

‚ÄúIt‚Äôs all a drick!‚Äù exclaimed Mrs.¬ÝSieppe, with sudden conviction. ‚ÄúI ain‚Äôt no fool; dot‚Äôs nothun but a drick.‚Äù

‚ÄúWell, of course, mamma,‚Äù exclaimed Trina, ‚Äúit‚ÄôsÔªø‚Äî‚Äù

But Mrs.¬ÝSieppe put her head in the air.

‚ÄúI‚Äôm too old to be fooled,‚Äù she persisted. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs a drick.‚Äù Nothing more could be got out of her than this.

The party stayed to the very end of the show, though the kinetoscope was the last number but one on the programme, and fully half the audience left immediately afterward. However, while the unfortunate Irish comedian went through his ‚Äúact‚Äù to the backs of the departing people, Mrs.¬ÝSieppe woke Owgooste, very cross and sleepy, and began getting her ‚Äúthings together.‚Äù As soon as he was awake Owgooste began fidgeting again.

‚ÄúSave der brogramme, Trina,‚Äù whispered Mrs.¬ÝSieppe. ‚ÄúTake ut home to popper. Where is der hat of Owgooste? Haf you got mein handkerchief, Trina?‚Äù

But at this moment a dreadful accident happened to Owgooste; his distress reached its climax; his fortitude collapsed. What a misery! It was a veritable catastrophe, deplorable, lamentable, a thing beyond words! For a moment he gazed wildly about him, helpless and petrified with astonishment and terror. Then his grief found utterance, and the closing strains of the orchestra were mingled with a prolonged wail of infinite sadness.

‚ÄúOwgooste, what is ut?‚Äù cried his mother eyeing him with dawning suspicion; then suddenly, ‚ÄúWhat haf you done? You haf ruin your new Vauntleroy gostume!‚Äù Her face blazed; without more ado she smacked him soundly. Then it was that Owgooste touched the limit of his misery, his unhappiness, his horrible discomfort; his utter wretchedness was complete. He filled the air with his doleful outcries. The more he was smacked and shaken, the louder he wept.

‚ÄúWhatÔªø‚Äîwhat is the matter?‚Äù inquired McTeague.

Trina‚Äôs face was scarlet. ‚ÄúNothing, nothing,‚Äù she exclaimed hastily, looking away. ‚ÄúCome, we must be going. It‚Äôs about over.‚Äù The end of the show and the breaking up of the audience tided over the embarrassment of the moment.

The party filed out at the tail end of the audience. Already the lights were being extinguished and the ushers spreading druggeting over the upholstered seats.

McTeague and the Sieppes took an uptown car that would bring them near Polk Street. The car was crowded; McTeague and Owgooste were obliged to stand. The little boy fretted to be taken in his mother‚Äôs lap, but Mrs.¬ÝSieppe emphatically refused.

On their way home they discussed the performance.

‚ÄúIÔªø‚ÄîI like best der yodlers.‚Äù

‚ÄúAh, the soloist was the bestÔªø‚Äîthe lady who sang those sad songs.‚Äù

‚ÄúWasn‚ÄôtÔªø‚Äîwasn‚Äôt that magic lantern wonderful, where the figures moved? WonderfulÔªø‚Äîah, wonderful! And wasn‚Äôt that first act funny, where the fellow fell down all the time? And that musical act, and the fellow with the burnt-cork face who played ‚ÄòNearer, My God, to Thee‚Äô on the beer bottles.‚Äù

They got off at Polk Street and walked up a block to the flat. The street was dark and empty; opposite the flat, in the back of the deserted market, the ducks and geese were calling persistently.

As they were buying their tamales from the half-breed Mexican at the street corner, McTeague observed:

‚ÄúMarcus ain‚Äôt gone to bed yet. See, there‚Äôs a light in his window. There!‚Äù he exclaimed at once, ‚ÄúI forgot the doorkey. Well, Marcus can let us in.‚Äù

Hardly had he rung the bell at the street door of the flat when the bolt was shot back. In the hall at the top of the long, narrow staircase there was the sound of a great scurrying. Maria Macapa stood there, her hand upon the rope that drew the bolt; Marcus was at her side; Old Grannis was in the background, looking over their shoulders; while little Miss Baker leant over the banisters, a strange man in a drab overcoat at her side. As McTeague‚Äôs party stepped into the doorway a half-dozen voices cried:

‚ÄúYes, it‚Äôs them.‚Äù

‚ÄúIs that you, Mac?‚Äù

‚ÄúIs that you, Miss Sieppe?‚Äù

‚ÄúIs your name Trina Sieppe?‚Äù

Then, shriller than all the rest, Maria Macapa screamed:

‚ÄúOh, Miss Sieppe, come up here quick. Your lottery ticket has won five thousand dollars!‚Äù
